vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Parameter for Sending ICMP Redirects on all IPv4 Interfaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matching_list" fi done #

triage_decision.reason: Disabling ICMP redirects via a sysctl is a standard, low-risk configuration change that does not affect authentication, authorization, or critical services. It follows STIG hardening patterns for networking parameters and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.

We also need to add file maybe /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf with line net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0 if not present.

That outputs exit 0 or 1 but no output. We can check via run_cmd. Then if not exist, we use write_file to create file. But requirement says using only read_file to inspect before modifying. For presence check, we could use read_file to attempt reading; if error? Might fail. We'll first read_file, if empty output or error? Hmm read_file may error if file doesn't exist? We must handle? Could try anyway; if not exist, run_cmd cat? But use read_file; if error, returns something. We'll just attempt read_file; if returns null or empty, then create file.

Also need to ensure /etc/sysctl.conf may have line for send_redirects; we must comment out or modify. So read_file /etc/sysctl.conf. If line exists, we need to comment or set. Use sed -i 's/^\s*net\.ipv4\.conf\.all\.send_redirects\s*=.*$/net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0/'.

And we must ensure the line is added if no exist. But we already create a file /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf with the line, so that should override. So minimal change: just create that file.

Thus plan: 1) Use run_cmd to check existence of /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf, but must not chain. So run_cmd "test -f /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf". Check exit code. We'll use that. If test fails (exit 1), then create the file via write_file. We must include content "net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0\n". Set mode 0644.

Also in case file exists, but maybe has different value; read_file it and confirm. If contains line not equal 0, use sed -i to modify. But we can't chain sed within function, we do one command. We can run sed -i to modify line.

We must read_file to check. So read_file system again.

But requirement: Always use read_file to inspect before modifying. So we should use read_file. If file not exist (empty response?), we treat as missing. Then write file. If exists, read content; if missing or wrong. Use write_file to replace.

Similarly, maybe /etc/sysctl.conf contains line; but systemd-sysctl will override by priority? /etc/sysctl.d files have higher precedence than /etc/sysctl.conf. So we can ignore.

Thus plan: read_file, determine. Then run_cmd to sed modify if needed? But editing file must use sed -i. But we can also use write_file. Use write_file simpler. We'll override file.

Thus plan summary: Read file; if missing or content not exactly "net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0", then write file with that line.

Also test runtime: run_cmd "sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects" then set to 0? But we don't need runtime change? It's okay, verification may check persistent. We can set runtime also with sysctl -w but not part of rules? Description says run command for runtime. We could run run_cmd "sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0". That ensures immediate. Not required but good.

Thus final plan: 1) read_file /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf. 2) If output != "net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0" (maybe trailing newline). Then write_file. 3) Run command sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0. But use run_cmd one at a time.

Thus proceed: Send plan to review.

review_feedback: The provided script only comments out existing sysctl statements but does not set the value to 0 either at runtime or in /etc/sysctl.d, so the system may remain vulnerable if the default is 1.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The provided script only comments out existing sysctl statements but does not set the value to 0 either at runtime or in /etc/sysctl.d, so the system may remain vulnerable if the default is 1.
concerns: Does not apply the desired change (0) at run‑time; instead relies on the system default, which is often 1.  Without a persistent addition the setting may revert after reboot.  The regex may miss lines with leading whitespace or comments, causing incomplete coverage.  Potentially modifies system config files unnecessarily.  No verification step to ensure the value is actually 0 after execution.
suggested_improvements: Add an explicit sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0 command to set the runtime value.  Add a line net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0 to a dedicated /etc/sysctl.d/ file (e.g., /etc/sysctl.d/90-disable-icmp-redirects.conf) to ensure persistence.  Use a loop to create or modify that file rather than commenting out all existing lines.  Include a verification step to confirm the value is set.  Consider handling the case where the parameter is already present in /etc/sysctl.conf or /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf by overwriting it instead of commenting out.
